El Oasis · History & Culture · Pre-Columbian Series

Alto del Cardal & Tierra Blanca

Excavaciones arqueológicas del Volcán Irazú · J. Fidel Tristán, 1913–1914

In late July 1913 and again across 27 July–1 August 1914, the Costa Rican naturalist and archaeologist José Fidel Tristán (1874–1932) conducted a series of excavations and surface surveys on the southwestern flank of Volcán Irazú, Cartago Province — principally on the properties of José Marín at a locality called Alto del Cardal, and on the adjoining lands of Jorge Gómez at Alto de Tierra Blanca, near the Río Reventado. This sub-catalogue gathers the surviving field photographs, Tristán's own manuscript diary pages, and his illustrated artifact inventory from that campaign, cross-referenced against the institutional finding aid for the Fondo José Fidel Tristán at the Archivo Nacional de Costa Rica.

The material documents stone-slab cist tombs (tumbas de cajón) containing tripod ceramic vessels, bundled secondary burials, and carved stone grave goods, alongside two large anthropomorphic effigy metates recovered from the surrounding pasture — one from Marín's land, one from Gómez's — both photographed and anatomically annotated by Tristán himself.

Provenance & revision note An earlier working draft of plate CAR-01 (the burial stereo-view) was provisionally catalogued under a different place name and region — "Alto del Pardo," Moín, Limón Province — based on a single difficult reading of the card's handwritten caption. Closer comparison against Tristán's own diary (Section C, below) confirms the correct locality as Alto del Cardal, Cartago, and the earlier attribution has been withdrawn. See the revision flag on CAR-01 for the full account.

I. The Burial

The principal field photograph documenting an opened stone-slab cist tomb in situ, with its ceramic and skeletal contents undisturbed at time of exposure.

Sepultura indígena, Alto del Cardal · Indigenous Burial, Alto del Cardal

CAR-01
Date29 July 1913
LocalityAlto del Cardal (Casa de Marín), Cartago
PhotographerJ. Fidel Tristán (attrib.)
FormatStereo photograph, glass-plate negative print

A handwritten caption along the card's right margin reads, in period script: "Sepultura indígena. Alto del Cardal. (Marín). 29 de Julio de 1913." The autograph initials at the close of the caption are abraded and not confidently legible; given the secure attribution of the place name to Tristán's documented 1913–14 Cardal campaign (Section III, below), the most probable reading is J.F.T. (José Fidel Tristán), though this remains a visual judgment rather than a certainty.

The photograph shows a rectangular stone-slab cist (tumba de cajón) opened to expose its contents in situ, prior to removal. A human cranium rests tilted within a flaring tripod ceramic bowl at center-left; a second tripod vessel beside it holds several slender objects — possibly worked bone or stone tools — standing upright in the fill. At least five long bones (femora and tibiae) are arranged in a tight, matching-orientation row against the rear slab, a regularity suggesting a deliberate secondary/bundled placement rather than disturbance. Two rounded stones, one a well-formed sphere, sit among the bones as probable grave furniture.

The slab-cist architecture and plain tripod vessel forms are broadly consistent with the Curridabat and Cartago phases (c. 300–1550 CE) of the Central Highlands ceramic sequence, and with the prevalence of box-tomb cemeteries documented at Alto del Cardal in later archaeological literature. Surface decoration and slip color cannot be determined from this monochrome print, so a specific ceramic-type attribution (e.g. Cartago Línea Roja, Irazú Línea Amarilla) is not asserted.

Condition / processing Original scan ref. 079355; contrast-enhanced and clarity-restored working copy (DxO PhotoLab DeepPRIME XD) used for this entry and for detail crops.
Revision note This plate was previously catalogued as EXC-M03 in a separate working file ("Excavaciones de Moín"), with the locality read as "Alto del Pardo" and the region identified as Moín, Limón Province. That reading was a provisional, unverified transcription of the same difficult handwriting addressed here; no Moín-related diary entry, archival record, or independent source was ever found to support it, and Tristán's own diary places him at Cartago/Irazú on the surrounding dates. The Moín attribution should be considered withdrawn and superseded by this entry.

II. The Monoliths

Two large anthropomorphic effigy metates (ceremonial grinding stones), recovered from pasture land on two adjoining properties and photographed by Tristán with anatomical annotation. These are distinct objects from distinct findspots and should not be conflated.

Monolito indígena, terreno de José Marín · Effigy Metate, Marín Property

CAR-02 – CAR-05
Date29–30 July 1913
LocalityAlto del Cardal, terreno de José Marín, Cartago
Object typeAnthropomorphic effigy metate (grinding stone / possible altar)
Four viewsCAR-02 / 03 / 04 / 05, this set

Tristán's diary (Section III, page 3) describes this object directly: a hard-stone monolith representing a misshapen, broad human body, with a basin or trough (batea) forming part of the figure itself. He records male genitalia in relief — penis with glans and testicles — a humped vertebral column on the reverse, a developed abdomen and navel on the front, and a basin measuring three decimeters in diameter. He had been told by local informants that the stone had stood among the corral stones for many years, its original context already lost.

Tristán himself was uncertain of its function — diary text poses the open question of whether it served as an altar or as a maize-grinding stone — and that ambiguity is preserved here rather than resolved retrospectively.

Field context Photographed in the open pasture at Alto del Cardal, evidently after excavation/clearing rather than in situ; the surrounding tree-line and slope match other Cardal-locality photographs in this set.

Vista 2 · View 2

CAR-03

Second view of the same monolith, showing the dorsal/vertebral aspect of the figure.

Vista 3 · View 3

CAR-04

Third view, showing the basin (batea) and lateral profile of the figure.

Vista 4 · View 4

CAR-05

Fourth view, field context with the monolith standing in pasture grass, tree-line visible behind.

Monolito indígena, terreno de Jorge Gómez · Effigy Metate, Gómez Property

CAR-10
DateJuly 1914
LocalityAlto de Tierra Blanca, terreno de Jorge Gómez, próximo al Río Reventado
AnnotatorJ. Fidel Tristán (autograph captions)
Distinct fromCAR-02–05 (Marín monolith)

Tristán's own annotated photo-collage, four views mounted on a single sheet (frente / espalda / lado de la batea / batea — front / back / basin-side / basin), with anatomical labels in his hand: cabeza, glande, pene, testículos on the front; columna vertebral on the back; ombligo at the navel; batea and ano on the basin face. Captioned: "Monolito indígena, descubierto en terrenos de Jorge Gómez — Alto de Tierra Blanca, próximo al Río Reventado, por J. Fid. Tristán — Julio 1914."

This is a separate object from the Marín-property monolith (CAR-02–05) — a different findspot, a different landowner, and documented a full year later in Tristán's 1914 campaign. The two should not be merged or treated as alternate views of a single artifact.

III. The Field Diary

Tristán's manuscript notebook, "Notas arqueológicas del Volcán Irazú" (1914), narrates the Cardal and Tierra Blanca campaign in his own hand and is the primary documentary source corroborating the place names, dates, and findings catalogued above. Full transcription is pending; key passages are summarized below pending translation.

Notas arqueológicas del Volcán Irazú · Archaeological Notes on Volcán Irazú

DIARY
AuthorJ. Fidel Tristán
Span of entries27 July – 1 August 1914
Signed/datedSan José, 16 August 1914
Pages catalogued here12 (partial — title page through closing signature)

27–28 July: Departs San José with "Ester" in poor weather. Visits the elderly Jorge Gómez, who describes old boundary pillars (mojones) marking the limits of the Cofradía de la Virgen de los Ángeles lands at Tierra Blanca, and recalls that a French collector, Lacroix, had already dug there in 1912.

28–29 July: Surveys the Alto de Tierra Blanca terrace, noting abundant potsherds (tiestos) in a long-cultivated maize field. Opens two numbered burials; the first had already been disturbed, the second yields a small ordinary pot and fragmentary human bone.

30 July: Travels to "Las Huacas" via Alto del Cardal (Casa de Marín). Describes and photographs the Marín-property monolith (CAR-02–05) in detail, and notes clear evidence of older, previously opened burials nearby. Records that Marín's land had already been extensively looted in earlier years, frustrating efforts to find an intact tomb — though numerous fragments of well-carved stone objects (idols, heads, grinding stones) were recovered from the disturbed fill. Expresses regret that this material was never systematically collected for the national museum.

31 July: Moves to Jorge Gómez's land northwest of Tierra Blanca, with the owner's son accompanying. Opens four numbered cist tombs. Sepultura No. 2 yields a seated monkey figure, a jaguar/feline head, several long bones, a vessel containing a small turtle-effigy grinding stone, and human cranial fragments. Sepultura No. 3 yields a pair of idols joined back-to-back and a sooted cooking pot. Sepultura No. 4 is left unopened for lack of time.

1 August: Returns to San José. Notes that sepultura clusters and isolated burials are reported "from time to time" throughout the wider Tierra Blanca/Cardal area, and records his intention to explore the region further. Signed and dated at close: "San José, 16 de Agosto de 1914. J. Fid. Tristán."

Note on this summary The above is a condensed paraphrase of twelve manuscript pages for catalogue purposes; it is not a verbatim transcription. A full transcription/translation, if undertaken, should supersede this summary.

IV. Artifact Inventory — "Antigüedades del Irazú"

Tristán's own illustrated catalogue of stone and clay objects recovered across the 1913–14 campaign, organized by findspot. Reproduced here as a research aid pending full transcription; original page numbering (his own) is preserved in the descriptions below.

A) Objetos de piedra: Alto del Cardal · Stone Objects: Alto del Cardal

CATALOGUE

Sixteen numbered pen-and-ink sketches of stone objects from the terreno de José Marín: a rough head fragment (#1), a carved face fragment (#2), a feline/"tiger" head fragment (#3), anthropomorphic torso fragments with hands clasped behind the back (#5–7), and a series of grinding-stone (piedra de moler) fragments (#8–14), concluding with a fragment described as the "foot of an idol" (#16).

B) Objetos de arcilla · Clay Objects

CATALOGUE

Five sub-groups, numbered #17 through #89: (1) vessels from the cist tomb Tristán personally opened; (2) sherds collected across the Marín and Borloz terrenos, including one fragment with an incised design he compares to wares seen at San Isidro; (3) a "gift from Marín" group of complete miniature tripod vessels — several zoomorphic (armadillo, feline, bird forms) — explicitly noted as having been kept in the Marín household and used as children's toys; (4) two stone celts found on the "calzada india"; (5) further sherds from land east of the Marín house; and a closing set from Tierra Blanca proper, including two full anthropomorphic figurines (#77–78), a tripod bowl (#79), and the twin back-to-back idols matching Sepultura No. 3 in the diary narrative (#68).

V. Field & Display Photographs

Additional photographs from the same campaign: excavation-party portraits, an offering-pit detail, and specimen-display images of smaller carved stone objects. Placement within the narrative sequence above is provisional.

Grupo de excavación · Excavation Party

CAR-07

A group portrait on the excavated hillside: two men with digging tools, three boys, and a woman holding a shovel, posed around the spoil heap. Likely taken at Tierra Blanca during the 31 July 1914 session described in the diary.

Dos hombres junto a la fosa · Two Men at the Trench

CAR-08

Two men seated at the rim of an open excavation, shovels planted beside them; a small assemblage of vessels and bone is visible at the base of the trench. Companion image to CAR-07.

Pozo de ofrenda · Offering Pit

CAR-09

Wide view down into a narrow pit, fern roots penetrating the fill; at base, a small figurine, a vessel, and several worked bone or stone tools laid out in situ. Possibly corresponds to a sepultura described in the diary's Tierra Blanca section.

Detalle del pozo de ofrenda · Offering Pit, Detail

CAR-06

Closer detail of a pit base showing two small carved stone or clay figures side by side, a shallow bowl, and worked bone/wood fragments. Likely the same or an adjoining feature to CAR-09.

Cabezas de piedra, exhibición · Carved Heads, Display

CAR-11

Nine small carved stone heads and figures arranged for photographic record on a flat slab. Likely corresponds to several of the numbered "cabeza" sketches in Section IV's stone-object inventory.

Cabeza tallada, presentación · Carved Head, Presentation

CAR-13

A single carved stone head set on a wooden chair for photographic record, with a young woman and a hatted man — likely members of the Marín or Gómez household — looking on.

Losa perforada · Perforated Stone Slab

CAR-12

A boy stands beside a flat stone slab with a single drilled perforation, propped against a tree trunk. Function undetermined — possibly a door-stone or grinding implement; not yet matched to a specific diary passage.

VI. Archival Reference

Fondo José Fidel Tristán — Documento No. 27

ARCHIVAL
RepositoryArchivo Nacional de Costa Rica
Accession rangeFotografías No. 78.560 – 78.576 (17 unidades)
ScopeAspecto arqueológico y cementerios indígenas del Volcán Irazú
Cataloguing date2002

The institutional finding-aid cover sheet for this photograph group within the Fondo José Fidel Tristán, confirming the regional scope as Volcán Irazú — independently corroborating the Cardal/Tierra Blanca attribution over the earlier, since-withdrawn Moín reading. The sheet notes the photographs were rehoused in Album No. 1 of Tristán's photographs for preservation.

This document does not provide item-level descriptions for each of the 17 photographs in the range, so individual plates in this catalogue (CAR-01 etc.) cannot yet be matched one-to-one against specific accession numbers within 78.560–78.576. That mapping remains an open task for future archival research.
